messages.content: Activation dropped 12% WoW. I've uploaded our Amplitude funnel data, three Gong transcripts from the last two weeks, and our last two sprint retros. I need to understand what caused this and what we should do about it. I have a leadership sync in 15 minutes.

Got it — I'm processing all three documents now. While that runs, quick clarifying question: is the 12% drop in overall activation or in a specific segment? And do you know yet if it's a tracking issue, seasonality, or a real behavioral change?
It's overall activation. Not tracking — we already sanity checked. Looks real. Biggest drop seems to be in organic acquisition, but I haven't isolated it yet.

Finished processing. Here's what I found across your three sources:

**Amplitude:** The 12% overall drop is driven almost entirely by a 31% drop in organic acquisition users. ICP users (active job seekers, searching 3+ times per week) show only a 4% drop. Non-ICP users (passive, low-intent) are down 38%.

**Gong transcripts:** 2 of 3 interviews surface friction at the same funnel step — users landing from Google search hitting a mandatory sign-up wall before seeing any job results.

Verbatim from User 2: 'I just wanted to see if there were any jobs before bothering to sign up. When it asked me to log in first I just left.'

**Sprint retro:** Your team shipped a change 11 days ago that tightened the sign-up gate on organic landing pages to reduce unqualified applications. The change was intended for low-intent users but applies to all organic traffic regardless of search intent signal.

**Hypothesis:** The activation drop is primarily caused by the sign-up gate change applied to organic traffic 11 days ago. It is disproportionately impacting low-intent users from generic search queries, but the friction is broad enough to reduce overall activation. This is not a tracking or seasonality issue.

provenance.excerpt: Organic acquisition -31% WoW. ICP segment -4%. Non-ICP -38%. Drop onset: 11 days ago.
User 2: 'I just wanted to see if there were any jobs before signing up. When it asked me to log in first I just left.'
Shipped: sign-up gate tightened on organic landing pages to reduce unqualified applications. No intent signal scoping.

messages.content: That tracks. The gate change was meant to reduce junk applications but we didn't scope it narrowly enough. What should we do?

Based on the evidence, I'd suggest two experiments rather than a rollback:

**Experiment 1 (High confidence):** Scope the sign-up gate by search intent signal. Users arriving from high-intent queries (job title + location, specific company name) see job results before the gate. Users from low-intent queries (generic 'jobs near me') hit the gate earlier. This preserves the quality goal while recovering organic activation.

**Experiment 2 (Medium confidence):** Show a preview of 3 relevant job cards before the sign-up prompt for all organic traffic. Verbatim from User 2 and User 3 both suggest that seeing relevant results is the conversion trigger — not the sign-up itself.

I'll hold off on Experiment 2 as a confirmed recommendation until I see whether the preview approach has been tested before. Do you have any prior experiment data on gated vs. ungated previews I should factor in?
We ran a preview experiment about 6 months ago — it increased sign-ups but conversion to application was lower. I'll share that doc.

That's the key constraint. With that prior experiment on record, Experiment 1 is the higher-confidence bet — it addresses the root cause directly without the conversion risk the preview approach introduced.

I've noted the prior experiment as a constraint node in your workspace so it surfaces automatically in any future experiment design conversation.

Shall I generate the three deliverables now?

Here are your three deliverables, built from the data you provided. Sections marked as provisional can be sharpened by adding persona detail or connecting your analytics tool.

Open any artifact to review and edit — or tell me what you want to adjust and I'll update it directly.

artifacts.title: Metric Diagnostic Summary
artifacts.description: What dropped, by how much, in which segments, and why it's real.
artifacts.content:
**Metric:** New job seeker activation rate (first 7 days)
**Drop:** -12% WoW (overall); -31% organic acquisition segment

**ICP impact:** -4% (Active Job Seekers, high-intent, searching 3+ times/week)
**Non-ICP impact:** -38% (Passive Job Seekers, low-intent, generic query organic)

**Tracking / seasonality:** Ruled out — sanity check confirmed with data team prior to session.

**Key pattern:** Drop onset correlates precisely with sprint deployment 11 days ago. No other significant product or infrastructure changes in the same window.

**Confidence:** High — 3-source corroboration: Amplitude funnel data + Gong interview transcripts + sprint retrospective notes.

artifacts.title: Root Cause & Opportunity Assessment
artifacts.description: The causal chain from signal to root cause, with business impact estimate.
artifacts.content:
**Root cause (high confidence):** Sign-up gate change applied to all organic traffic on day -11. Gate was scoped to reduce unqualified applications from low-intent users, but the implementation applies globally regardless of search intent signal.

**Supporting evidence:**
- Amplitude: Drop onset matches deployment date exactly. Organic segment -31%, ICP segment only -4%.
- Gong User 2: 'I just wanted to see if there were jobs before signing up. When it asked me to log in first I just left.'
- Gong User 3: Similar friction at same funnel step — gate encountered before any job results are visible.
- Sprint retro: Gate change confirmed as primary shipped item in last sprint.

**Business impact:** If organic acquisition represents ~40% of new user volume, a sustained 31% drop in that segment projects to 12-15% reduction in overall new user growth monthly if unaddressed.

**Unknowns:** Long-term retention impact on users who do convert through the gate. Availability of real-time intent signal data for gate scoping — needs engineering confirmation before sprint commit.

artifacts.title: Action Plan with Focused Bets
artifacts.description: Recommended next moves based on the root cause assessment, with effort estimates and success criteria.
artifacts.content:
**Recommended Action:** Experiment 1 — Intent-scoped sign-up gate

**Hypothesis:** Scoping the sign-up gate to activate only for low-intent organic traffic (generic queries) while showing results-first for high-intent queries will recover organic activation without increasing unqualified applications.

**Variant:** Gate activates only for generic queries (e.g. 'jobs near me'). High-intent queries (job title + location, company name) see results before the gate.

**Control — Current state:** Sign-up gate activates for all organic traffic regardless of search intent.

**Target metric:** New job seeker activation rate, organic acquisition segment
**Success threshold:** Recover to within 5% of pre-change baseline within 2 weeks
**Guardrail metric:** Application quality score — must not decrease

**Effort:** Engineering M — intent signal already exists in the system; gate logic change is additive, not a rebuild
**Design:** S — gate UX unchanged, only trigger condition changes

**Constraint noted:** Prior preview experiment (6 months ago) showed higher sign-up rate but lower conversion to application. Preview approach excluded from this recommendation on that basis.

○ Provisional — strengthens with confirmed intent signal availability from engineering. Recommend 30-min sync with Engineering Lead before sprint commitment.

○ Provisional — success threshold based on organic representing ~40% of new users. Validate against current Amplitude baseline before using in leadership presentation.

**Next step:** 30-minute sync with Engineering Lead to confirm intent signal availability and gate architecture before committing to sprint.

title: What do we know about creator analytics demand?

messages.content: What do we know about creator analytics demand? Give me the full picture.

Here's everything I've found across your NPS data, interviews, support tickets, and metrics:

**By the numbers:**
- 68 NPS survey responses in Q1 explicitly requesting a creator analytics dashboard
- 41 support tickets in March reporting that analytics data isn't visible in the creator UI
- 14/14 creator interviews surfaced analytics as a top 3 pain point
- Analytics NPS volume grew 40% over the last 6 weeks — the fastest-growing request in the dataset

**What creators are actually asking for:**
The NPS data shows three clusters: (1) impressions and reach by post — 'I can't see who my content is reaching,' (2) time-of-day performance — 'I don't know when to post,' and (3) follower growth tracking — 'I can't see if I'm growing.'

**The context that makes this urgent:**
Substack launched creator dashboards in April 2026. In your creator interviews from February, Substack was mentioned in 9 of 14 sessions as a platform creators were 'considering moving to.' That was before the dashboard launch — the pull is likely stronger now.

Your April 8 team sync data adds a critical data point: Power Creators (50K+ followers) are churning at 3x the rate of Q4 2025, and 34% of exit surveys cite analytics as the primary reason.

**What's not yet in the data:**
I don't have direct A/B test data on analytics feature adoption or conversion lift. If that exists in your analytics warehouse, surfacing it would strengthen the case significantly.

provenance.excerpt: 68 requests for analytics dashboard. Top themes: impressions visibility, time-of-day performance, follower growth tracking.
14/14 interviews surfaced analytics. Substack mentioned in 9/14 as alternative. Churn risk: high.
41 support tickets March 2026. Engineering confirmed: data exists in backend, not surfaced in UI.
Power Creator churn 3x rate. 34% exit survey: analytics gap primary reason.

title: What changed in our roadmap in Q1 and why?

messages.content: What changed in our roadmap in Q1 and why? Walk me through the full sequence.

Here's the sequence of changes Mira reconstructed from your Jira history, planning notes, and executive signals:

**December 2025: Creator Fund Beta deprioritized**
Creator Fund Beta was moved from Q1 to H2 2026. The trigger was a legal review that flagged complexity around payment processing classification. Engineering capacity was also a factor — the team was already committed to Post Scheduling v2. Decision made by: Engineering Lead + Legal. No user demand data influenced this — it was a pure capacity and compliance call.

**January 2026: Q2 locked on Post Scheduling v2**
Q2 planning (January 8) locked Post Scheduling v2 as the primary Q2 bet. Rationale at the time: it was the highest-volume NPS request and the lowest-complexity build. Creator Analytics was considered but deferred — the reasoning was 'let's do the quick win first.' At this point, analytics NPS volume was 41 requests — not yet the spike it became.

**March 2026: Executive priority shift**
March 3 CEO all-hands: 'Creator Tools needs a revenue signal in Q3.' March 5: VP Revenue moved Newsletter Monetization from Q4 to Q3. This was the biggest mid-quarter shift. It created the prioritization tension that still exists today — Q3 now has two competing mandates.

**The tension that wasn't resolved:**
The January Q2 planning decision assumed Creator Analytics could wait. But by March, analytics NPS volume had grown 40% and Substack had announced their dashboard launch. The March executive shift didn't account for the emerging analytics urgency. That gap is the core of the Q3 prioritization problem you're now navigating.

provenance.excerpt: Creator Fund Beta moved to H2 2026. Reason: legal complexity, eng capacity. December 15, 2025.
January 8 planning: Post Scheduling v2 locked as Q2 primary. Rationale: highest NPS volume at the time, lowest complexity.
CEO mandate March 3 → VP Revenue moves Monetization Q4→Q3 on March 5.
Decision context: analytics NPS at 41 in January vs 68 by March — the spike came after planning locked.
